narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Saturday, June 13, 2026
Headlines
Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms are expected today, with the greatest severe weather threat arriving this evening.
A Slight to Enhanced Risk of severe weather exists across the region, with damaging winds as the primary concern.
Large hail, an isolated tornado, and localized flash flooding are also possible.
Cooler and drier weather returns Sunday and Monday before storm chances increase again next week.

Today will be an active weather day across Missouri and Illinois as several rounds of showers and thunderstorms move through the region. While not every location will experience severe weather, everyone should remain weather aware as conditions become favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms, particularly later this afternoon and evening.

The first round of thunderstorms is expected during the morning into early afternoon hours. These storms should remain relatively disorganized and carry only a limited severe weather threat. However, a few stronger cells could produce gusty winds and small hail before weakening as they move east across the region.

As temperatures warm and humidity increases this afternoon, additional isolated thunderstorms may develop ahead of an approaching cold front. The atmosphere will become increasingly unstable, allowing a few storms to become severe. Any stronger storms that develop during the afternoon could produce damaging wind gusts and hail up to quarter size. While the tornado threat remains low during this period, it cannot be completely ruled out.

The greatest severe weather threat arrives late this afternoon and continues through the evening and early overnight hours. A cold front moving southeast through Missouri and Illinois will trigger a more organized line of thunderstorms capable of producing widespread damaging wind gusts. Wind damage is expected to be the primary hazard, with some storms capable of producing winds in excess of 60 mph. Large hail remains possible, and isolated tornadoes cannot be ruled out, especially within any embedded rotating storms that develop along the front.

The highest severe weather risk will be across central and northeast Missouri, where storms will encounter the most favorable combination of instability and wind shear. As storms move southeast toward the St. Louis metro, southeast Missouri, and southwest Illinois during the evening and overnight hours, they are expected to remain strong, though confidence in widespread severe weather decreases somewhat farther south and east.

In addition to the severe weather threat, thunderstorms today will be capable of producing very heavy rainfall. Rainfall rates of one to two inches per hour are possible in the strongest storms. Fortunately, storm movement should remain fairly quick, limiting the potential for widespread flooding. However, locations that experience multiple rounds of thunderstorms could see localized flash flooding develop. This concern is greatest across portions of northeast Missouri, where recent heavy rainfall has already saturated the ground and elevated area streams and creeks.

By Sunday morning, the cold front will be south of the region, allowing cooler and much drier air to move in. High temperatures Sunday and Monday will be well below normal for mid-June, with comfortable humidity levels and abundant sunshine. The break from active weather will be brief, however, as warmer temperatures, increasing humidity, and additional chances for showers and thunderstorms return beginning Tuesday and continue through the middle of next week.

First Warn Severe Weather Threat Today

Threat Level: Slight to Enhanced Risk

Primary Threat: Damaging Wind Gusts 60-70+ mph

Secondary Threats: Large Hail and Isolated Tornadoes

Flood Threat: Localized Flash Flooding

Timing: Morning through Early Afternoon: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms with a limited severe weather threat.

Afternoon: Additional thunderstorms may develop with a few becoming strong to severe.

Late Afternoon through Early Overnight: Highest risk for organized severe thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front.


Multiple rounds of thunderstorms are expected across the region today, but the greatest concern comes this evening as a cold front moves through Missouri and Illinois. Damaging winds are expected to be the primary hazard, though large hail, isolated tornadoes, and localized flash flooding remain possible. Make sure you have multiple ways to receive warnings and stay weather aware through tonight.
